figures.title: Estimating the Stochastic Discount Factor from Option Prices and Predicting the Equity Premium
figures.caption: Figure 1 shows the time-series evolution of the daily EP for target maturities of 30, 60, 90, 120, 180, 270 and 360 — This figure displays time-series plots of equity premium (EP) estimates derived from stochastic discount factor specifications across multiple target maturities (30, 60, 90, 120, 180, 270, and 360 days). figures.title: Estimating the Stochastic Discount Factor from Option Prices and Predicting the Equity Premium
figures.caption: Figure 2. Estimated CVS SDF with Full-Sample Data — This figure displays seven line plots showing estimated Stochastic Discount Factors (SDFs) derived from constant volatility smile (CVS) models at different time horizons: 30, 60, 90, 120, 180, 270, and 360 days. Each plot has moneyness (log-return) on the x-axis ranging from approximately -2.0 to 2.0, and SDF values on the y-axis ranging from 0.0 to 2.5, with three colored curves (orange, blue, and green) representing different estimation speci

figures.title: Estimating the Stochastic Discount Factor from Option Prices and Predicting the Equity Premium
figures.caption: Figure 3. Estimated TVS SDF with Full-Sample Data — This figure displays seven line plots showing estimated Term Volatility Smile (TVS) Stochastic Discount Factor (SDF) across different time horizons: 30, 60, 90, 120, 180, 270, and 360 days. Each plot has moneyness (log-moneyness ratio) on the x-axis ranging from approximately -2.0 to 2.0, and SDF values on the y-axis ranging from 0.0 to 2.5, with three colored curves (orange, blue, and green) representing different specifications or scenarios.

figures.title: Dynamic Causal Portfolio Choice: Hedging the Rotation of the Common-Driver Manifold
figures.caption: Figure 1 shows the optimal policy as the two projected funds it decomposes into. The hedge decomposes further, and the distinction matters for the paper’s claim of novelty. Writing the transported motion in the frame of Theorem 4.5, ψt splits into a vertical part and a horizontal part, and the two are Gt-orthogonal by Theorem 4.5, so the split is well defined and not a matter of attribution. The v — This figure illustrates the decomposition of optimal hedging demand in a dynamic portfolio choice

figures.title: Dynamic Causal Portfolio Choice: Hedging the Rotation of the Common-Driver Manifold
figures.caption: Figure 1: The optimal policy (7) as two projected funds. The myopic fund projects the conditional premium onto the tangent-admissible space; the manifold hedge passes the predicted state motion ψt through the driver noise ΛZ, the sensitivity B(z), and the same projector MC. The hedge is nonzero when the information geometry is predictably moving. — This figure presents a block diagram of a portfolio construction framework with two components: a myopic tangent fund and a manifold hedge fund. The

figures.title: Dynamic Causal Portfolio Choice: Hedging the Rotation of the Common-Driver Manifold
figures.caption: Figure 2: The moving manifold (Section 3.3). With state-dependent sensitivities B(z) the tangent space TxMt rotates as the driver state Z flows. The intertemporal term hedges this predictable rotation of the geometry; in the fixed-geometry case the span does not move and only the premium does. — This diagram illustrates how a manifold's tangent space rotates as the underlying driver state Z evolves over time.
